expanded_description: This section synthesizes all five triangle congruence criteria—SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, and HL—into complete formal proofs. Students learn to analyze diagrams, identify which criterion applies, construct logical chains from given information through intermediate congruences to triangle congruence, and apply CPCTC to prove additional geometric relationships. Mastery of these proof patterns is essential for all subsequent geometry topics.

core_concept.principle: A triangle congruence proof follows a consistent logical structure: (1) State the given congruent parts, (2) Establish any additional congruent parts using definitions, postulates, and theorems (reflexive property, vertical angles, alternate interior angles, midpoint, angle bisector), (3) Prove the triangles congruent by citing the appropriate criterion (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL), (4) Use CPCTC to conclude any remaining corresponding parts are congruent. The strategic skill lies in selecting the right criterion and identifying the shortest logical path.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a triangle congruence proof as building a legal case. You have evidence (the given congruent parts) and you need to prove the triangles are identical beyond reasonable doubt. Sometimes the evidence directly fits a congruence criterion—an open-and-shut case. Other times you need to establish additional evidence—a shared side is congruent to itself (reflexive), vertical angles are equal, or parallel lines create equal alternate interior angles. Once you have three solid pieces of evidence matching SSS, SAS, ASA, or AAS, you present your case and win. Then CPCTC is your reward—it gives you access to prove anything else you need about the triangles.

image.alt: A flowchart showing the triangle congruence proof process: Given Information → Additional Congruences (Reflexive, Vertical Angles, Alternate Interior Angles, Midpoint, Angle Bisector) → Five Congruence Criteria (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, HL) → Triangle Congruence → CPCTC → Desired Conclusion. Each criterion has a mini-diagram showing the required parts.
image.caption: Triangle congruence proof flowchart: from givens through congruence to CPCTC.

sections.section_title: The Structure of a Triangle Congruence Proof
sections.introduction: Every triangle congruence proof follows the same logical architecture. Understanding this structure transforms proof-writing from a mysterious art into a systematic process. The key is recognizing which of the five criteria the given information points toward and what additional congruences are needed.

introduction_image.alt: A template for triangle congruence proofs divided into four sections: (1) Given Parts — what is stated in the problem. (2) Additional Parts — what can be derived (reflexive, vertical angles, etc.). (3) Congruence Criterion — which of SSS/SAS/ASA/AAS/HL is satisfied. (4) CPCTC — what additional conclusions follow. Each section illustrated with a diagram example.
introduction_image.caption: Proof structure: givens → additional congruences → criterion → CPCTC.

key_concept.definition: A triangle congruence proof has four logical segments: (1) The Given—congruent sides or angles stated in the problem. (2) Derived Congruences—additional equal parts established through the Reflexive Property (shared sides/angles), Vertical Angles Theorem, Alternate Interior Angles (parallel lines), Definition of Midpoint, Definition of Angle Bisector, or other theorems. (3) Criterion Application—the moment when three pairs of congruent parts are assembled to satisfy SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL. (4) CPCTC—the conclusion that any remaining corresponding parts are congruent.
key_concept.context: This structure is universal. Every triangle congruence proof, regardless of complexity, follows this pattern. Internalizing the structure allows students to approach any proof with confidence.

image.alt: A completed example proof with the four segments color-coded: Given (blue) — $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$, $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{CB}$. Derived (green) — $\overline{BD} \cong \overline{BD}$ (Reflexive). Criterion (orange) — SSS. CPCTC (purple) — $\angle A \cong \angle C$. Each segment labeled with its role.
image.caption: The four-part structure: Given, Derived, Criterion, CPCTC.

process_flow.process: Analyze the Diagram and List All Given Information
process_flow.description: Mark all given congruent parts on the diagram using tick marks and angle arcs. Identify which parts are explicitly stated as congruent in the problem.
process_flow.mechanism: Read the 'Given' statements carefully. For each given congruence, mark the diagram: single/double/triple tick marks for sides, single/double/triple arcs for angles. If a point is a midpoint, mark the two sub-segments congruent. If a ray bisects an angle, mark the two resulting angles congruent. If lines are parallel, mark the relevant alternate interior or corresponding angles as congruent.
process_flow.result: A fully marked diagram showing all given congruent parts.
applications: '$M$ is midpoint of $\overline{BC}$' → mark $\overline{BM}$ and $\overline{CM}$ with matching ticks.
'$\overline{AD}$ bisects $\angle BAC$' → mark $\angle BAD$ and $\angle CAD$ with matching arcs.

image.alt: A geometric diagram being marked up: given congruent sides get matching tick marks, given congruent angles get matching arcs, a midpoint creates two equal segments, an angle bisector creates two equal angles. The marked diagram is clear and complete.
image.caption: Step 1: Mark every given congruence on the diagram.

process_flow.process: Identify What Additional Congruences Can Be Derived
process_flow.description: Look for shared sides or angles (Reflexive Property), vertical angles, or other relationships that automatically create additional congruent pairs.
process_flow.mechanism: Check for: (1) A side shared by both triangles → Reflexive Property. (2) Intersecting lines → Vertical Angles are congruent. (3) Parallel lines with a transversal → Alternate Interior or Corresponding Angles are congruent. (4) A segment bisector → the two sub-segments are congruent. (5) An angle bisector → the two sub-angles are congruent. (6) Perpendicular lines → right angles are congruent. Add these derived congruences to the diagram.
process_flow.result: Additional congruent pairs identified and marked on the diagram.
applications: 'Two lines cross at $E$' → $\angle AEB \cong \angle CED$ (Vertical Angles).
'$\overline{AB} \parallel \overline{CD}$' → alternate interior angles at the transversal are congruent.

image.alt: Diagram with derived congruences added: a shared side marked 'Reflexive,' vertical angles marked with matching arcs and 'Vertical Angles Thm,' parallel lines creating alternate interior angles marked 'Alt Int Angles.' Each derived congruence highlighted.
image.caption: Step 2: Derive additional congruences from the diagram's structure.

process_flow.process: Select the Congruence Criterion and Prove Triangles Congruent
process_flow.description: Count the congruent parts. Match them to the pattern of SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL. Write the triangle congruence statement with vertices in corresponding order.
process_flow.mechanism: Examine the three (or more) pairs of congruent parts now established. Determine which criterion they satisfy: three sides → SSS. Two sides and included angle → SAS. Two angles and included side → ASA. Two angles and non-included side → AAS. Hypotenuse and leg of right triangles → HL. Write the congruence statement carefully with vertices in corresponding order. Cite the criterion as the reason.
process_flow.result: The proven statement: $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$ by [criterion].
applications: 'Two sides and included angle: $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$, $\angle B \cong \angle E$, $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{EF}$ → SAS.'

image.alt: The marked diagram with three pairs of congruent parts highlighted and connected to the appropriate criterion: SSS, SAS, ASA, or AAS. A decision flowchart: 'Count your pairs → What pattern do they form? → That's your criterion.'
image.caption: Step 3: Match the congruent pairs to a criterion and prove triangles congruent.

process_flow.process: Apply CPCTC for Any Remaining Conclusions
process_flow.description: Once triangle congruence is established, use CPCTC to prove that any additional corresponding parts are congruent. This is the final goal of most congruence proofs.
process_flow.mechanism: After the triangle congruence statement, write the specific conclusion needed: a segment congruence, an angle congruence, or a relationship like a midpoint or perpendicular. Cite 'CPCTC' as the reason. This step transforms the general triangle congruence into the specific geometric fact the problem asked you to prove.
process_flow.result: The proof's final conclusion, justified by CPCTC.
applications: '$M$ is the midpoint — CPCTC and definition of midpoint.' (Proving a midpoint.)

image.alt: The final step: from $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$, an arrow labeled CPCTC points to the specific conclusion $\angle A \cong \angle D$ (or whatever is needed). The conclusion is boxed as the proof's target.
image.caption: Step 4: Use CPCTC to prove the specific conclusion required.

image.alt: Complete proof structure overview: the four-step process, a sample proof traced through all four steps, and a summary checklist for each step.
image.caption: Triangle Congruence Proof Structure: a systematic four-step process for any congruence proof.

sections.introduction: With five available criteria, selecting the right one is a critical strategic decision. The choice depends on which parts are given or can be easily derived. This section provides decision-making strategies and common diagram patterns associated with each criterion.

introduction_image.alt: A decision tree: 'What congruent parts do you have?' → '3 sides?' → SSS. '2 sides + angle?' → 'Is angle included?' → Yes → SAS. No → 'Can you prove the third angle?' → Yes → AAS. '2 angles + side?' → 'Is side included?' → Yes → ASA. No → AAS. 'Right triangle?' → 'Hypotenuse + leg?' → HL.
introduction_image.caption: Choosing a congruence criterion: match your congruent parts to the pattern.

key_concept.definition: Criterion selection is based on the type and position of congruent parts: SSS (three sides), SAS (two sides + included angle), ASA (two angles + included side), AAS (two angles + non-included side), HL (hypotenuse + leg, right triangles only). The most efficient proof uses the criterion that matches the given information most directly, minimizing the need for derived congruences.
key_concept.context: Strategic criterion selection is a key proof-writing skill. The best choice is the one that requires the fewest additional logical steps. Sometimes multiple criteria could work—choose the simplest path.

image.alt: Five common diagram patterns, each associated with a criterion: (1) Kite/quadrilateral with shared diagonal → SSS. (2) Angle bisector in isosceles triangle → SAS. (3) Parallel lines with transversal → ASA. (4) Two angles given + any side → AAS. (5) Right triangles with shared hypotenuse → HL.
image.caption: Common diagram patterns and their natural congruence criteria.

worked_examples.title: Selecting and Applying the Correct Criterion
worked_examples.problem:
For each, identify which criterion proves the triangles congruent and write a complete two-column proof.

1) Given: $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$, $\overline{AD}$ bisects $\angle BAC$. Prove: $\triangle ABD \cong \triangle ACD$.
2) Given: $\overline{PQ} \parallel \overline{SR}$, $\overline{PS} \parallel \overline{QR}$. Prove: $\triangle PQS \cong \triangle RSQ$.
3) Given: $\angle X \cong \angle Y$, $\overline{XZ} \cong \overline{YZ}$. Prove: $\triangle XWZ \cong \triangle YWZ$ where $W$ lies on $\overline{XY}$ and $\overline{ZW} \perp \overline{XY}$.

step_by_step.explanation:
Given $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$ (side), $\overline{AD}$ bisects $\angle BAC$ → $\angle BAD \cong \angle CAD$ (angle), $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{AD}$ (reflexive side). Two sides and the included angle → SAS.

1) $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$ — Given
2) $\overline{AD}$ bisects $\angle BAC$ — Given
3) $\angle BAD \cong \angle CAD$ — Def. of Angle Bisector
4) $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{AD}$ — Reflexive Property
5) $\triangle ABD \cong \triangle ACD$ — SAS

Parallel lines → alternate interior angles at both ends of the shared diagonal $\overline{QS}$.

1) $\overline{PQ} \parallel \overline{SR}$ — Given
2) $\angle PQS \cong \angle RSQ$ — Alternate Interior Angles
3) $\overline{QS} \cong \overline{SQ}$ — Reflexive Property
4) $\overline{PS} \parallel \overline{QR}$ — Given
5) $\angle PSQ \cong \angle RQS$ — Alternate Interior Angles
6) $\triangle PQS \cong \triangle RSQ$ — ASA

Given $\overline{ZW} \perp \overline{XY}$ → $\angle XWZ$ and $\angle YWZ$ are right angles → $\triangle XWZ$ and $\triangle YWZ$ are right triangles. Given $\overline{XZ} \cong \overline{YZ}$ (hypotenuse) and $\overline{ZW} \cong \overline{ZW}$ (reflexive leg). → HL.

1) $\overline{ZW} \perp \overline{XY}$ — Given
2) $\angle XWZ$ and $\angle YWZ$ are right angles — Def. of Perpendicular
3) $\triangle XWZ$ and $\triangle YWZ$ are right triangles — Def. of Right Triangle
4) $\overline{XZ} \cong \overline{YZ}$ — Given
5) $\overline{ZW} \cong \overline{ZW}$ — Reflexive Property
6) $\triangle XWZ \cong \triangle YWZ$ — HL

Alternatively, $\angle X \cong \angle Y$ (given), $\angle XWZ \cong \angle YWZ$ (both 90°), $\overline{XZ} \cong \overline{YZ}$ → AAS.

image.alt: Three diagrams with complete two-column proofs: (1) SAS with angle bisector, (2) ASA with parallel lines, (3) HL with perpendicular right triangles.
image.caption: Selecting criteria: SAS, ASA, and HL applied to common diagram patterns.

worked_examples.final_answer: See step-by-step for complete proofs using SAS, ASA, and HL.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Strategic criterion selection based on given information and diagram configuration.

sections.introduction: Mastery of triangle congruence proofs requires practice with all five criteria, various diagram configurations, and the complete four-part proof structure. Work through these problems to build comprehensive proof-writing fluency.

worked_examples.title: Triangle Congruence Proof Practice
worked_examples.problem:
Write complete two-column proofs.

1) Given: $M$ is midpoint of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$. Prove: $\triangle AMC \cong \triangle BMD$.
2) Given: $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$, $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{DA}$. Prove: $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA$.
3) Given: $\angle P \cong \angle Q$, $\overline{PR} \cong \overline{QR}$. Prove: $\triangle PRS \cong \triangle QRS$ where $S$ is on $\overline{PQ}$ and $\overline{RS} \perp \overline{PQ}$.

step_by_step.step: 1) SAS with midpoints and vertical angles
step_by_step.explanation:
1) $M$ is midpoint of $\overline{AB}$ — Given
2) $\overline{AM} \cong \overline{MB}$ — Def. of Midpoint
3) $M$ is midpoint of $\overline{CD}$ — Given
4) $\overline{CM} \cong \overline{MD}$ — Def. of Midpoint
5) $\angle AMC \cong \angle BMD$ — Vertical Angles Theorem
6) $\triangle AMC \cong \triangle BMD$ — SAS

1) $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$ — Given
2) $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{DA}$ — Given
3) $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{CA}$ — Reflexive Property
4) $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA$ — SSS

1) $\overline{RS} \perp \overline{PQ}$ — Given
2) $\angle PRS$ and $\angle QRS$ are right angles — Def. of Perpendicular
3) $\triangle PRS$ and $\triangle QRS$ are right triangles — Def. of Right Triangle
4) $\overline{PR} \cong \overline{QR}$ — Given (hypotenuse, actually—wait, $\overline{PR}$ is a leg? In right triangle $\triangle PRS$ with right angle at $S$, the hypotenuse is $\overline{PR}$ if the right angle is at $S$. Yes, $\overline{PR}$ is opposite the right angle, so it's the hypotenuse. Similarly $\overline{QR}$ is the hypotenuse of $\triangle QRS$.)
5) $\overline{RS} \cong \overline{RS}$ — Reflexive Property (leg)
6) $\triangle PRS \cong \triangle QRS$ — HL

image.alt: Three diagrams with complete proofs: (1) SAS with midpoints and vertical angles, (2) SSS with reflexive diagonal, (3) HL with perpendicular right triangles.
image.caption: Triangle congruence proof practice: SAS, SSS, and HL in two-column format.

worked_examples.final_answer: See step-by-step for complete two-column proofs with all statements and reasons.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Complete triangle congruence proofs using SAS, SSS, and HL with midpoints, vertical angles, reflexive property, and perpendicular lines.

summary: Triangle congruence proofs follow a universal four-part structure. Key points: 1) Proof Structure: Given congruences → Derived congruences (Reflexive, Vertical Angles, Alternate Interior, Midpoint, Angle Bisector) → Criterion Application (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, HL) → CPCTC conclusion. 2) Criterion Selection: Match available congruent parts to the closest criterion. Three sides → SSS. Two sides and included angle → SAS. Two angles and included side → ASA. Two angles and non-included side → AAS. Right triangles with hypotenuse and leg → HL. 3) Derived Congruences: The Reflexive Property (shared sides/angles), Vertical Angles Theorem, and Alternate Interior Angles (parallel lines) are the most common sources of additional congruent pairs. 4) CPCTC: Used only AFTER triangle congruence is established. Proves that remaining corresponding parts are congruent. 5) The systematic four-step approach transforms proof-writing from guesswork into a reliable process applicable to any congruence problem.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Triangle Congruence Proofs' in center. Branches: Proof Structure (givens → derived → criterion → CPCTC), Five Criteria (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, HL with diagrams), Derived Congruences (reflexive, vertical angles, alternate interior, midpoint, angle bisector), CPCTC (final step, proves remaining parts), Strategic Selection (matching parts to the best criterion).
review_questions_image.caption: Proofs with Triangle Congruence Review: the complete four-part proof structure and all five criteria.
